1: The oracle is explicitly dated to Jeremiah's imprisonment (asur bachatzer hamatara, confined in the court of the guard — cf. 32:2-3). The temporal marker 'a second time' (shenit) links this oracle to the previous (ch. 32's land-purchase) as a second confirming word of restoration. Hope spoken from prison is more credible than hope spoken from freedom; the circumstances contradict the message, which forces the question of its source.
2: koh-amar YHWH osah zaot YHWH yotzer otah lehachinah YHWH shemo (kōh-ʾāmar Yhwh ʿōśeh zōʾt Yhwh yōṣēr ʾōtāh lĕhăḵînāh Yhwh šĕmô): 'Thus says the LORD who made the earth, the LORD who formed it and established it — the LORD is his name.' Three creation verbs — asah (made), yatzar (formed, the potter's verb of Gen 2:7 and Isa 45:18), hachin (established/fixed) — ground the restoration promise in creative authority. The same God who formed the earth can reform the nation. YHWH shemo (the LORD is his name) is the doxological formula asserting exclusive divine identity (cf. Amos 5:8; 9:6; Isa 42:8).
3: qra elai ve'e'enecha ve'agidah lecha gedolot vebetzurot lo yada'ta (qrāʾ-ʾēlay wĕʾeʿĕnekā wĕʾaggîdāh lĕkā gĕdōlôt ûbĕṣūrôt lōʾ yādāʿtā): 'Call to me and I will answer you, and will tell you great and hidden things that you have not known.' This is the only verse in Jeremiah where YHWH directly invites prayer. Gedolot (great things) and betzurot (hidden/fortified/inaccessible things) are revelation beyond ordinary knowledge. Batzur shares a root with the word for fortress/cliff (batzor): these are things 'fortified away' from human access, revealed only by divine disclosure (cf. Deut 29:29: 'the hidden things belong to the LORD our God, but the revealed things belong to us and to our children forever'). Jesus's invitation 'Ask and it will be given you, seek and you will find, knock and the door will be opened' (Matt 7:7) extends this promise to everyone in the new covenant.
4: The houses torn down (hetusim) and the siege ramps (solelot) built against them — the very material of the city's destruction is being used as raw material for the city's defense. The oracle speaks of judgment (v. 5) and restoration (v. 6) in tight sequence — the same God who hides his face (v. 5) will reveal health and healing (v. 6).
5: ba'im lhilachem et-haKasdim umale otam et-pigrye ha-adam asher hiketi be-api uvachamati: 'The Chaldeans are coming to fight and to fill those houses with the corpses of men whom I struck down in my anger and wrath.' YHWH's 'hiding his face' (histarti panai) from the city is the covenant-withdrawal formula: the withdrawal of divine presence, more catastrophic than any military defeat. The phrase appears in the 'hidden face' texts of Deut 31:17-18 and Isa 54:8 — a covenantal concept rather than a mere absence.
6: hineni ma'aleh la arukha umarpeh verapatim vegalitilahem atzumat shalom ve'emet (hinnĕnî maʿăleh lāh ʾărûkāh ûmarpēʾ ûrĕpāʾtîm wĕgillêtî lāhem ʿaṣûmat šālôm wĕʾĕmet): 'Behold, I will bring health and healing to it; I will heal them and reveal to them an abundance of prosperity and security.' Three restoration terms: arukah (new skin/healing, specifically wound-closure — cf. 8:22 where there is no healing), marpeh (cure/medicine), and shalom ve-emet (peace and faithfulness). The medical vocabulary reverses the wound imagery of the book: the incurable wound (30:12-13) will be healed. The divine healer of 30:17 ('I will restore your health and heal your wounds') is here specified with the language of abundant prosperity.
7: veshavti et-shevut Yehudah ve'et shevut Yisrael uvenetim otam ke-varishonah: 'I will restore the fortunes of Judah and Israel and rebuild them as they were at the beginning.' Shavti shevut (restore the fortunes) — the idiom, literally 'I will turn the turning,' appears at the center of restoration promises throughout Jeremiah (29:14; 30:3, 18; 31:23; 32:44; 33:11, 26). The Hebrew construction uses the infinitive absolute of shuv (return) to express the completeness of the reversal. Both Judah and Israel — the divided kingdoms — will be restored together, a promise that encompasses all twelve tribes.
8: vetihartim mikol avonam asher chate'u li vesalachti lekol avonoteihem asher chate'u li: 'I will cleanse them from all the iniquity by which they sinned against me, and I will forgive all the iniquity and rebellion by which they sinned against me.' The double-forgiveness formula: tihar (cleanse/purify — the priestly term for ritual purification, Lev 13-14) and salach (forgive/pardon — the judicial/covenantal term; only YHWH is ever the subject of salach in the OT). Two different dimensions of sin: avon (iniquity, the guilt and liability of sin) and pisha (rebellion, the active willful defiance). Cleansing addresses the defilement; forgiveness addresses the guilt. Both are promised. This anticipates the new covenant's permanent forgiveness of 31:34 ('I will remember their sin no more') and the NT's 'forgiveness of sins and cleansing from all unrighteousness' (1 John 1:9).
9: vehayetah li leshem lesason ulitehillah uletiferet: 'This city will be to me a name of joy, a praise, and a glory before all the nations.' The three terms — shem (name/reputation), sason (joy), tehillah (praise), tiferet (glory/beauty) — are the covenant blessings inverted from the curses. Deut 28:37 pronounced Jerusalem would become a mashal (proverb) and shenina (byword) of shame. Here it becomes a name of joy and praise — the exact opposite. The nations shuddering (pachadu) at YHWH's goodness inverts their watching Jerusalem's destruction with schadenfreude (cf. Lam 1:7; 2:15-16).
10: The double designation of desolation — 'without man or beast' (ein adam ve-ein behema) — repeats the judgment formula of 32:43 and anticipates its reversal. The 'cities of Judah and streets of Jerusalem' are the spaces where the covenant community's life was lived — commercial, domestic, and liturgical. The oracle promises that those same spaces will be refilled with joy.
11: qol sasson veqol simchah qol chatan veqol kallah (qôl śāśôn wĕqôl śimḥāh qôl ḥātān wĕqôl kallāh): 'The voice of joy and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride.' This four-fold 'voice of' formula appears in both judgment (7:34; 16:9; 25:10: 'I will silence the voice of joy ... the voice of the bridegroom and bride') and restoration (33:11). The liturgical fragment embedded in the verse — hodu et-YHWH Tzvaot ki tov YHWH ki le-olam chasdo (Give thanks to the LORD of hosts, for the LORD is good, for his steadfast love endures forever) — is the refrain of Ps 136 and 1 Chronicles 16:34. The Psalmic doxology of the temple liturgy will be heard again: the silenced song of 7:34 is restored.
12: The desolate land reoccupied by shepherds and flocks represents the return of ordinary pastoral life — a sign of covenant normalcy. The shepherd-and-flock image has been used throughout Jeremiah for covenantal relationships (ch. 23; cf. Ezek 34). Its return signals that the covenant community is functioning again under proper care.
13: ba-arei hahar ba-arei hashfela uve-arei hanegev uve-eretz Binyamin uvsvivot Yerushalaim: The geographic catalog — hill country, Shephelah, Negev, Benjamin, Jerusalem environs — represents all of Judah's territory, from north to south, from the highlands to the lowlands. The sheep passing under the shepherd's hands to be counted (ya'avru al-yadei moneh) echoes Ezek 20:37 ('I will make you pass under the rod' — a shepherd's counting method) and implies a covenantal census: every sheep accounted for, none lost. This imagery directly informs Jesus's parable of the shepherd who counts and seeks the one lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7).
14: hineh yamim baim neum YHWH vahaqimoti et-hadavar hatov asher dibarti el-beit Yisrael ve'al-beit Yehudah: 'The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will fulfill the good promise I made to the house of Israel and the house of Judah.' Hadavar hatov (the good word/promise) — the specific content follows in vv. 15-18. This is a meta-promise: YHWH promises to keep his previous promises, establishing a second level of covenantal assurance. The phrase yamim baim (days are coming) is Jeremiah's standard eschatological marker, pointing beyond the immediate historical horizon.
15: bayamim hahem uveet hahi atzmich leDavid tzemach tzaddiq (bYyāmîm hāhēm ûbāʿēt hahîʾ ʾaṣmîaḥ lĕdāwid ṣemaḥ ṣaddîq): 'In those days and at that time I will cause a righteous Branch to spring up for David.' The Branch-oracle of 23:5 is repeated almost verbatim — the second occurrence establishes this as Jeremiah's central messianic promise, worthy of repetition. Tzemach (branch/shoot) appears as a messianic title again at Zech 3:8 ('my servant the Branch') and 6:12 ('the man whose name is the Branch, and he will branch out from his place and build the temple of the LORD'). The branching-out image carries growth and organic life: the dead stump of the Davidic line will sprout again, as in Isa 11:1 ('a shoot from the stump of Jesse').
16: beyamim hahem tivasha Yehudah viYerushalaim tishkon lavetch veze asher yiqra lah YHWH Tzidqenu (bYyāmîm hāhēm tiwwāšēaʿ yĕhûdāh wîrûšālaim tiškon lābeṭaḥ wĕzeh ʾăšer yiqrāʾ-lāh Yhwh ṣidqênû): 'In those days Judah will be saved and Jerusalem will dwell in safety. And this is the name by which it will be called: 'The LORD is our righteousness.'' Note the crucial shift from 23:6: there, the King is named YHWH Tsidqenu; here, the city Jerusalem receives that name. The community takes on the king's name and character — the theological principle that the people are defined by who rules them. When the Righteous Branch reigns, his righteousness becomes the city's defining characteristic. Rev 21:2-3 captures this: the new Jerusalem coming down from God, 'the dwelling of God is with man' — the city and its king are inseparable.
17: ki koh-amar YHWH lo yikaret leDavid ish yoshev al-kise beit Yisrael (kî kōh-ʾāmar Yhwh lōʾ-yikkārēt lĕdāwid ʾîš yōšēb ʿal-kissēʾ bêt yiśrāʾēl): 'For thus says the LORD: There will never fail to be a man from David's line to sit on the throne of the house of Israel.' Lo yikaret leDavid ish (there will not be cut off from David a man) echoes the unconditional form of the Davidic covenant in 2 Sam 7:16 ('your house and your kingdom will endure forever before me'). After the Jehoiachin curse of 22:30 ('record this man as childless'), this reaffirmation of the Davidic line is remarkable: the curse on the throne-succession through natural descendants does not annul the covenant promise fulfilled in a different way.
18: velakkohanim halevi'im lo-yikaret ish milfanai ma'aleh olah umaqtir minchah: 'And the Levitical priests shall never lack a man in my presence to offer burnt offerings, to burn grain offerings, and to make sacrifices continually.' The pairing of Davidic kingship (v. 17) and Levitical priesthood (v. 18) in the eternal covenant points toward the NT's union of the two offices in Christ. Hebrews' central argument is precisely the fulfillment: Jesus is both the Davidic king (Heb 1:3-5) and the Melchizedekian high priest (Heb 4:14-5:10; 7:1-28) who supersedes the Levitical priesthood. The inseparability of the two offices promised here is achieved in Christ's single person.
19: The messenger formula introducing the covenant-guarantee oracle. The shift from restoration promises (vv. 1-18) to the cosmic-covenant argument (vv. 20-26) is indicated by a fresh divine word — YHWH is not merely promising but arguing from creation order for the reliability of what has been promised.
20: koh amar YHWH im-tafiru et-beriti hayom ve-et beriti halaylah (kōh ʾāmar Yhwh ʾim-tāpērû ʾet-bĕrîtî hayyôm wĕʾet-bĕrîtî hallaylāh): 'Thus says the LORD: If you can break my covenant with the day and my covenant with the night, so that day and night no longer come at their appointed time.' YHWH uses creation order as collateral for covenant promise: the regularity of day and night (moadam, their appointed times) is YHWH's covenant with creation (cf. Gen 8:22: 'as long as the earth endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night will never cease'). The argument is a fortiori: only if creation's most fundamental rhythm can be disrupted will the Davidic promise fail. Paul uses similar cosmological argument for the security of divine election in Rom 8:38-39 (nothing in all creation can separate from God's love in Christ Jesus).
21: gam-beriti tufer et-David avdi: 'Then my covenant with my servant David could also be broken.' The logical chain: cosmic covenant (day/night) → Davidic covenant → Levitical covenant. All three are bound in a single covenantal fabric. The phrase avdi David (my servant David) is the royal honorific — the same title given to Moses (Deut 34:5), Joshua (Josh 24:29), Isaiah (Isa 20:3), and supremely to the messianic servant of Isa 52:13. It is never given to a reigning king in Jeremiah — it is the title of the idealized king of promise.
22: kasher lo-yisafer tzeva hashamayim velo yimad chol hayam ken arbeh et-zera David avdi ve'et-haLevi'im meshartai oti: 'As the host of heaven cannot be numbered and the sand of the sea cannot be measured, so I will multiply the offspring of David my servant and the Levites who minister to me.' The innumerable-offspring promise echoes the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 15:5: stars; 22:17: stars and sand; 32:12: sand). Its application to David's line and the Levites connects the messianic promise to the founding covenantal promise: the Davidic fulfillment is the Abrahamic fulfillment.
23: The messenger formula signals a new divine communication — a response to a specific objection being raised in the community (v. 24). The oracle addresses those who are drawing theological conclusions from the exile that undermine the covenant promises.
24: halo ra'ita mah ha-am hazeh dibberu lemor shnei hamishpachot asher bachar YHWH bahem vayim'asem: 'Have you not noticed what these people are saying? 'The LORD has rejected the two families he chose.'' The 'two families' are the Davidic dynasty and the Levitical priesthood — the two institutions just promised in vv. 17-18. The exile has generated a theology of divine rejection: if YHWH has abandoned Jerusalem, perhaps the covenants are void. The prophetic response does not deny the judgment but disputes the conclusion: discipline ≠ rejection.
25: koh amar YHWH im-lo beriti yomam valaylah chuqot shamayim va-aretz lo samti: 'Thus says the LORD: If I have not established my covenant with day and night and the fixed orders of heaven and earth.' Chuqot shamayim va-aretz (the fixed orders of heaven and earth) — the same language as 'the fixed laws of heaven and earth' in v. 25, referring to the natural laws of creation. These are the visible, daily, undeniable evidence of covenant-keeping: every sunrise is YHWH keeping his covenant with day; every night that ends is YHWH keeping his covenant with night. Creation-faithfulness is the argument for covenant-faithfulness.
26: ki-eshuv et-shevutam verichhamtim: 'I will restore their fortunes and have mercy on them.' The chapter closes with the restoration formula, now grounded in the cosmic-covenant argument. The descendants of Jacob and David cannot be ultimately rejected because the rejection of the covenants would require the dismantling of creation itself — and the daily evidence of day and night argues against that. Racham (mercy/compassion — from rechem, womb) is the covenant-loyalty term that expresses YHWH's most intimate commitment to his people: parental, physiological, irreversible.
